items.q: What is the main function of dietary fibre?
items.correct: Aiding digestion and promoting bowel health.
items.why: Students think all carbohydrates give energy. Fibre is a carb humans can't digest, so it provides NO direct energy. It bulks stools, slows glucose absorption, and feeds gut bacteria.

items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: Are local foods always more sustainable than imported ones?
items.correct: Not always - production methods can outweigh transport emissions.
items.misconception: Yes - shorter distance always means lower emissions.
items.random_wrong: No - imported food is always better.
items.why: Students apply 'shorter is better' too simply. A heated UK greenhouse tomato may have higher emissions than a Spanish field tomato trucked north. Lifecycle analysis matters more than miles alone.
The answer examiners want is: Fat. “Carbohydrate” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's 30 g. “10 g” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 4 kcal. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
